races.name: La Flèche Wallonne Féminine
races.description: The 29th edition of La Flèche Wallonne Féminine, the second of three Ardennes Classics this week. Both start and finish in Huy, with the Mur de Huy climbed twice in the final third of the race. The Mur de Huy (1.3km @ 9.6% avg, max ~18.8%) is one of the most feared finishes in women's racing. Anna van der Breggen won it seven consecutive times between 2015 and 2021; Demi Vollering claimed it in 2023; Kasia Niewiadoma-Phinney in a rain-soaked 2024; and Puck Pieterse outsprinted Vollering on her debut here in 2025. The decisive section is always the last 400m of the Mur where the gradients are most savage — riders who time their kick perfectly win here, those who launch too early burn out on the final ramps, while those who wait too long get pipped on the line. A one-puncheur kind of race.

key_climbs.name: Côte d'Ereffe
key_climbs.notes: Tackled twice on the circuit. Selection climb.

key_climbs.name: Côte de Cherave
key_climbs.notes: Stiff climb immediately before the Mur descent into Huy.

key_climbs.name: Mur de Huy (summit finish)
key_climbs.notes: The race is decided here. Two ascents — one before the final and the decisive summit finish. The S-bend at halfway features the most brutal gradients (>20% max in places). Inside the final 300m, the gradient eases slightly but legs are already burning. The last to launch a sprint rarely wins.

races.rider_notes: Puck Pieterse (Fenix-Premier Tech) is defending champion. Demi Vollering (FDJ United-SUEZ) is arguably the heavier favourite — she's won Omloop and Tour of Flanders this spring and has 1 win + 5 podiums in 7 career starts here. Kasia Niewiadoma-Phinney won this in 2024 and is a constant Mur de Huy threat. Pauline Ferrand-Prévot (Visma | Lease a Bike, 2014 winner) is a danger if she goes earlier than expected. Anna van der Breggen (SD Worx-Protime, record 7 wins) makes a return — she has not raced here since her 2021 win. Paula Blasi (UAE Team ADQ, Amstel Gold Race Ladies winner) will test if her form extends to the Mur. Liane Lippert (Movistar) has 1 podium and 4 top-10s here — her best climbs in the Ardennes. Also watch: Elise Chabbey (FDJ, domestique/captain), Noemi Ruëgg (EF-Oatly, climber), Magdeleine Vallières (EF-Oatly, World Champion), Karlijn Swinkels (UAE Team ADQ), Femke de Vries (Visma | Lease a Bike).

broadcasts.notes: TNT Sports 1 and HBO Max. Broadcast focuses on the final section, not from km 0. Women's race finishes after the men (who end ~16:00 CEST) — UK coverage expected mid-to-late afternoon.
Peacock (NBC Sports). ASO race — Peacock rights, not FloBikes.
FloBikes carries Flèche Wallonne in Canada.
Free live coverage on VRT Max (Flemish) and RTBF Auvio (French-speaking). Home country has full coverage.
Free coverage on NOS.
Free on France TV Sport.
Free on SBS on Demand — one of the few major spring classics with free ANZ coverage.
Eurosport and HBO Max across most European markets.

races.name: La Flèche Wallonne
races.description: The 90th edition of La Flèche Wallonne — the Walloon Arrow — second of the three Ardennes Classics. Starting from Herstal (a new host city for 2026, north-east of Liège), the race covers exactly 200km with 11 categorised climbs before finishing atop the Mur de Huy for the 42nd time since the summit finish was introduced in 1985. The route traverses rolling Ardennes terrain, briefly crosses into Luxembourg, then builds through three circuits in the Huy finale: each featuring the Côte d'Ereffe, Côte de Cherave, and the notorious Mur de Huy. Alejandro Valverde holds the record with five wins; the last French winner was Julian Alaphilippe in 2021. The 2026 edition is conspicuously missing its defending champion Tadej Pogačar (targeting Liège), Remco Evenepoel (recovering for Liège after Amstel), and Matteo Jorgenson (broken collarbone). That leaves a genuinely open race with no dominant favourite — the most unpredictable edition in years. Paul Seixas (19yo) enters as nominal favourite after his Itzulia Basque Country triumph.

key_climbs.name: Côte de Trasenster
key_climbs.notes: First major climb inside 50km. Sets the tone.

key_climbs.name: Côte d'Ereffe
key_climbs.notes: Tackled three times on the finishing circuit. Summit 18.5km from the line each time. Provides some selection but not the race-decider.

key_climbs.name: Côte de Cherave
key_climbs.notes: Hard climb with descent directly into the Mur de Huy. Positioning battle begins here.

key_climbs.name: Mur de Huy (summit finish)
key_climbs.notes: Three ascents: at ~130.8km, ~168km, and the decisive final finish. The S-bend halfway up contains the race's most savage gradients (18.8-20%+). The last 300m ease slightly but provide no relief. Riders must begin their sprint before the top to have any hope. The race is almost always decided here — the last non-summit winner was Astarloa in 2003 via a solo breakaway.

notable_absentees.reason: Targeting Liège-Bastogne-Liège (4th title). 2025 winner skipping to focus on Sunday's Monument.
Confirmed DNS — prioritising recovery after Amstel Gold Race win for Liège-Bastogne-Liège.
Broken collarbone sustained at Amstel Gold Race.

races.rider_notes: Paul Seixas (Decathlon CMA CGM, 19yo) is the headline favourite — Tour of the Basque Country GC winner and already marked for Liège. This is his Ardennes debut as a professional; he's unproven at the Mur but his climbing power makes him the logical pick. Kévin Vauquelin (INEOS Grenadiers) has finished 2nd at Flèche the past two years despite crashes this week at Amstel — he knows exactly how to ride the Mur. Romain Grégoire (FDJ) showed excellent form with 4th at Amstel and has good Flèche history (7th x2). Lenny Martinez (Bahrain, 22) was 4th last year and won a stage at Paris-Nice this season. Benoît Cosnefroy (UAE, 3rd at Amstel, 2nd and 4th at previous Flèches) is in strong form. Mattias Skjelmose (Lidl-Trek) has a Flèche pedigree (2nd in 2023) but failed to finish the last two editions. Christian Scaroni (XDS Astana) and Tobias Halland Johannessen (Uno-X) are dangerous outsiders. Former winners Julian Alaphilippe (2021), Marc Hirschi (2020), Dylan Teuns (2022) are all in the field. Pello Bilbao (Bahrain) and Ion Izagirre (Cofidis) add depth.

broadcasts.notes: Live from 11:45 BST (= 12:45 CEST) on TNT Sports 1 and HBO Max. Race starts 11:50 CEST — UK broadcast picks up mid-race. TNT Sports plans from £25.99/month standalone.
Live on Peacock from 06:30 ET (= 12:30 CEST). ASO-organised race = Peacock rights in USA, NOT FloBikes. From $10.99/month.
FloBikes carries Flèche Wallonne for Canadian viewers.
Free live coverage in the host country: VRT Max (Flemish) and RTBF Auvio (French). Full coverage from the start.
NOS provides free live coverage.
Free on France TV Sport and/or L'Équipe TV.
Free on SBS on Demand. Rare free coverage of a Spring Classic in Australia.
Sky Sport NZ.
Eurosport and HBO Max across most European markets. Some countries (Norway, Sweden, Denmark etc.) have local broadcasters.

data_confidence.notes: 90th edition confirmed. Route: Herstal → Huy, 200km confirmed (velowire: 'exactly 200 kilometers'; Yahoo Sports confirms 200km). New host city Herstal confirmed. Mur de Huy three times confirmed. Start time 11:50 CEST per Domestique. UK broadcast 11:45 BST confirmed per Cycling Weekly and ProCyclingUK. US Peacock confirmed — ASO race, not FloBikes. Canada FloBikes confirmed. Belgium VRT/RTBF free confirmed. France France TV/L'Équipe confirmed. Australia SBS on Demand confirmed (unusual free coverage for a WT Classic). NZ Sky Sport confirmed. Key absentees: Pogačar (confirmed targeting LBL), Evenepoel (confirmed DNS per Cyclingnews and IDL), Jorgenson (broken collarbone confirmed). Vauquelin (INEOS) starting despite crash at Amstel per IDL start list.

races.name: Tour of the Alps – Stage 3
races.finish_type: Flat to slightly downhill finish after long descent
races.description: The longest stage of the 2026 Tour of the Alps (174.5km, 3,626m climbing) — a demanding mountain test with a deceptive finish. Starting in Latsch/Laces in South Tyrol, the stage opens with 26 flat kilometres in the Val Venosta/Vinschgau before attacking the major climbing work. The centrepiece is the Hofmahdjoch/Passo Castrin (22.2km @ 5.7%), a lengthy Cat.1 climb that marks the highest point of the entire 2026 Tour of the Alps. This is followed by the Andalo/Lago di Molveno climb (14.4km @ 5.4%), then a long descent through Terme di Comano/Ponte Arche. A technical finale includes the Passo del Ballino, a descent to Lago di Tenno, a closing circuit with a Tenno bonus sprint, and then a descent to Riva del Garda before the finish in historic Arco (home of Sport Alto Garda, the race organiser). Despite the huge mountain work, the flat/downhill run-in to Arco may allow GC riders to regroup — this stage could be a breakaway day, with attacks on the Hofmahdjoch, and GC rivals watching each other.

gc_after_stage_2.source: Official Tour of the Alps race site / Cyclingnews

standings.notes: Stage 2 winner, takes Green Jersey. Sprint from small group on Martell summit.
Runner-up on Stage 2 sprint. Was poised to win solo before Pellizzari pounced.
Breakaway rider who held on all the way to the Martell summit for 3rd. Surprise podium.
Strong GC ride. The Colombian's comeback continues.
Bridged across late, attacked with 600m to go, caught by Bernal then Pellizzari used his wheel.

gc_after_stage_2.notes: Michael Storer (Tudor, defending champion) lost 19 seconds on Stage 2 — still in contention overall. Tom Pidcock cracked on the Martell final climb and lost significant time — confirmed he will not feature in the GC battle. Derek Gee (Lidl-Trek) also cracked. Tommaso Dati (UKYO, Stage 1 winner) was not a factor in Stage 2's climbers' finale.

key_climbs.name: Hofmahdjoch / Passo Castrin
key_climbs.notes: Highest point of the entire 2026 Tour of the Alps. Very long, sustained climb — over 1,200m of vertical gain. Summit well before the finish but takes a heavy toll on the legs.

key_climbs.name: Andalo / Lago di Molveno
key_climbs.notes: Second major climb. Less steep than Hofmahdjoch but still substantial.

key_climbs.name: Passo del Ballino
key_climbs.notes: Technical climb in the finale before descent to Lago di Tenno.

key_climbs.name: Tenno bonus sprint
key_climbs.notes: Time bonuses available at the closing circuit bonus point before the descent to Riva del Garda.

stages.result: Pellizzari (Red Bull) wins summit sprint. Arensman 2nd, Gaffuri 3rd. Storer -19s, Pidcock cracked.

stages.notes: TODAY. Longest stage. Hofmahdjoch central climb, flat/descent finish to Arco.
Queen stage. Passo Bordala (14.8km@6.9%) + Passo Redebus (5km@6.7%) + Bovo final ramp (1.6km@9.5%) inside final 10km.
Final stage. Nobls/Montoppio (12.7km@7%) twice on circuit, fast descent to Bolzano.

races.rider_notes: Stage 3's finish profile (big climbing work then long descent to Arco) makes a GC-defining move difficult in the final kilometres. This is likely a stage for a long-range breakaway with GC riders carefully watching each other over the Hofmahdjoch. Pellizzari's Red Bull team now controls the race. Arensman (INEOS) has the strongest recent form in this race (2nd overall last year, constantly dangerous). Bernal's climbing level has been impressive through Tour of the Alps' first two stages. Storer (Tudor) needs to limit further losses after Stage 2. Pidcock has been downgraded as a GC threat after cracking on Martell — IDL noted Pidcock confirmed he won't feature in LBL GC based on current condition.

broadcasts.notes: Live from 12:30 CEST (10:30 UTC) to 14:30 CEST per official race site. Race starts 9:55 CEST — broadcast is mid-race coverage window.
FloSports/FloBikes for US and Canada. FloBikes schedule shows Tour of the Alps at 10:45 UTC (12:45 CEST).
Eurosport (Europe and Asia) from 12:30 CEST per official race site.
RAI Sport expected for free in Italy. Race finishes in Arco, Trentino — home territory.

data_confidence.notes: Stage route Latsch/Laces → Arco, 174.5km (Cyclingnews/official) / 175.1km (official race news article) confirmed. Elevation 3,626m from PCS stage profiles. Start time 9:55 CEST per kolarsko.pl; IDL says 09:55. Finish ~14:20 CEST per kolarsko.pl. Official race site broadcast window confirmed as 12:30-14:30 CEST on Eurosport/HBO Max/TNT Sports/FloSports. FloBikes schedule confirms 10:45 UTC = 12:45 CEST for Tour of the Alps. GC after Stage 2 confirmed from official race site news article: Pellizzari leads, Arensman 2nd, Gaffuri 3rd, Bernal 4th, Vlasov 5th. Storer -19s confirmed from official race report.

podium: 1. Giulio Pellizzari (ITA, Red Bull-BORA-hansgrohe) — 2nd career pro win
3. Mattia Gaffuri (ITA, Team Picnic PostNL) — breakaway survivor
yesterday_result.notes: Race report from official site: nine-rider breakaway formed early, with Emanuel Zangerle collecting KOM points on Pillerhöhe before being caught on the Martell climb. Vlasov bridged late and attacked with 600m to go, Bernal chased, and Pellizzari emerged from Bernal's slipstream to sprint to victory. 'I came here three years ago and finished third on a stage. One year after I was second. Now I've finally won a stage here.' Pellizzari dedicated the win to his girlfriend's late father. Takes the Green (leader's) Jersey. Michael Storer (Tudor) lost 19 seconds — still in contention. Tom Pidcock and Derek Gee cracked on the final Martell climb.

news: Remco Evenepoel will NOT race La Flèche Wallonne — fully confirmed. Red Bull-BORA-hansgrohe confirmed on April 21. Focusing on Liège-Bastogne-Liège (Sunday April 26) rematch with Pogačar.
Tom Pidcock's GC prospects at Tour of the Alps are largely gone after cracking on Stage 2. He'll try to defend well for the remaining three stages. LBL remains his main target.
Giulio Pellizzari described Stage 2 as 'a statement ride' by the official race site — the 23-year-old Italian is now GC leader and the most consistent climber in this race.
La Flèche Wallonne Féminine has Puck Pieterse as defending champion but Demi Vollering (Omloop + Flanders winner this spring) is the broader favourite.

upcoming.notes: Arco → Trento, 167.8km. Queen stage with 4,111m climbing. Passo Bordala (14.8km@6.9%), Passo Redebus (5km@6.7%), and final sharp ramp to Bovo (1.6km@9.5%) inside last 10km. Race-defining stage for overall GC.
La Doyenne. 112th edition of the oldest Monument. Evenepoel vs Pogačar vs Seixas showdown anticipated. Also: Liège-Bastogne-Liège Femmes (10th edition). UK: TNT/HBO Max. USA: Peacock. CA: FloBikes.

issues: Flèche Wallonne women's start time: Yahoo Sports says '7:40 a.m. Eastern' = 13:40 CEST. ProCyclingUK says '12:40 BST' = 13:40 CEST. Both confirm the same. Women start after men in 2026 — unusual for Classics but confirmed by multiple sources.

Flèche Wallonne women's finish time: ProCyclingUK says '16:42-17:09 BST' = 17:42-18:09 CEST, which implies a 4+ hour race for 148.2km (~35km/h). This seems slow. IDL says 'after the men's finish (~16:30 CEST) you can watch the women's final hour' suggesting finish around 17:00-17:30 CEST. Using ~17:15 CEST as estimated finish.

Flèche Wallonne men's distance: Velowire confirms 'exactly 200 kilometers'. Yahoo Sports says '200-kilometer'. Domestique says '200km'. Official site historic data showed 205.5km (2025 edition). 2026 = 200km confirmed.
Tour of Alps Stage 3 distance: Cyclingnews lists 174.5km, official race news says 175.1km. Using 174.5km per PCS/Cyclingnews.
Vauquelin racing: IDL confirms he's starting despite crashing at Amstel. No indication of DNS.

SBS Australia for Flèche Wallonne: Cycling Weekly, Cyclingnews how-to-watch, and IDL all confirm SBS on Demand is free in Australia for Flèche Wallonne 2026 — this is unusual and notable for Australian viewers.

PCS today page cached (showing April 14 data). Cross-referenced against Cyclingnews calendar and FloBikes schedule. Three races confirmed for April 22: Flèche Wallonne Féminine (1.WWT), La Flèche Wallonne (1.UWT), Tour of the Alps Stage 3 (2.Pro). No other 1.1/2.1 races found.
